comparison_checkpoints.details: Confirm the customer host, Rocky 8 baseline, and build markers line up with the trusted lab appliance before deeper diagnosis.
comparison_checkpoints.label: Compare appliance identity first

comparison_checkpoints.details: Verify that the critical services below appear active in the customer environment and match the expected enabled state.
comparison_checkpoints.label: Compare critical service state

comparison_checkpoints.details: Use Apache proxy config and local port mappings to confirm the expected AlgoSec routes still point at the right local services.
comparison_checkpoints.label: Compare routed product endpoints

comparison_checkpoints.details: Use the listening-port count and service listener hints as a quick baseline for what should be reachable or bound on the appliance.
comparison_checkpoints.label: Compare visible listeners

decision_playbooks.decision_rule: If a step fails, stop the happy-path sequence, record that step as the current failure point, and collect the matching evidence before moving on.

dependency_path.details: Start here. Confirm httpd.service is active and ports 80 and 443 are listening before checking anything deeper.
dependency_path.label: Apache edge

dependency_path.details: If Apache is up, check disk space, inode usage, available memory, and recent OOM pressure on the host.
dependency_path.label: Host health

dependency_path.details: Then check the main UI services: keycloak.service on 8443 and ms-metro.service on 8080.
dependency_path.label: UI services

dependency_path.details: If the services are up, check httpd, keycloak, and ms-metro logs to find the real clue.
dependency_path.label: Logs

decision_playbooks.label: Appliance UI is down

steps.action: Check Apache first. Confirm httpd.service is running and ports 80 and 443 are open.
steps.decision_if_fail: Stop here and treat httpd.service or the 80/443 listener layer as the current failure point.
evidence_to_collect: Output of systemctl status for httpd.service, keycloak.service, and ms-metro.service.
Listener output for ports 80, 443, 8443, and 8080 from ss -lntp.
steps.if_fail: Stop here and treat httpd.service or the 80/443 listener layer as the current failure point.
steps.if_pass: If httpd is running and both ports are open, go to the next step.
steps.next_if_pass: If httpd is running and both ports are open, go to the next step.

recommended_commands.example_output:
● httpd.service - The Apache HTTP Server
   Loaded: loaded (/usr/lib/systemd/system/httpd.service; enabled; vendor preset: disabled)
   Active: active (running) since Sat 2026-03-07 13:37:12 EST; 2 weeks 2 days ago
 Main PID: 1018 (/usr/sbin/httpd)
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Unit is loaded and active (running).
recommended_commands.interpretation: If this is not active, treat the edge service itself as the failure point before going deeper.
recommended_commands.label: Check httpd.service status

recommended_commands.expected_signal: A listening socket is present for 80, 443.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If 80/443 are missing, the UI path is failing at the listener or bind layer.
recommended_commands.label: Check edge listeners

steps.why_this_matters: Apache is the front door for the UI. If Apache is down, the UI cannot load.

steps.action: Check system pressure. Confirm the server still has free disk, free inodes, and available memory.
steps.decision_if_fail: Stop here and treat host pressure as the current failure point.
evidence_to_collect: Output of systemctl status for httpd.service, keycloak.service, and ms-metro.service.
Listener output for ports 80, 443, 8443, and 8080 from ss -lntp.
steps.failure_point: Disk, inode, or memory pressure on the host
steps.if_fail: Stop here and treat host pressure as the current failure point.
steps.if_pass: If disk, inode, and memory checks look healthy, go to the service checks.
steps.next_if_pass: If disk, inode, and memory checks look healthy, go to the service checks.

recommended_commands.example_output:
Filesystem           Size  Used Avail Use% Mounted on
/dev/mapper/rl-root   60G   16G   45G  27% /
/dev/mapper/rl-data  238G   21G  218G   9% /data
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Main filesystems still have free space and are not close to 100% use.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If /, /boot, or /data is full or nearly full, services may stop writing logs, temp files, or data.
recommended_commands.label: Check disk usage

recommended_commands.example_output:
Filesystem          Inodes IUsed IFree IUse% Mounted on
/dev/mapper/rl-root    30M  343K   30M    2% /
/dev/mapper/rl-data   119M   21K  119M    1% /data
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Main filesystems still have free inodes and are not close to 100% inode use.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If inode use is high, services can fail even when normal disk space still looks free.
recommended_commands.label: Check inode usage

recommended_commands.example_output:
total        used        free      shared  buff/cache   available
Mem:           32Gi        13Gi       8.6Gi       2.4Gi         9Gi        15Gi
Swap:          24Gi          0B        24Gi
recommended_commands.expected_signal: The host still has available memory and is not fully relying on swap.
healthy_markers: Mem and Swap are shown in GiB
recommended_commands.interpretation: If available memory is very low or swap is heavily used, Java services may slow down, hang, or crash.
recommended_commands.label: Check memory pressure

recommended_commands.command: journalctl -k --since '-7 days' --no-pager | grep -i -E 'out of memory|oom|killed process' | tail -n 20
recommended_commands.expected_signal: No recent OOM lines are returned.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If OOM lines appear, the host has been killing or starving processes under memory pressure.
recommended_commands.label: Check for recent OOM pressure

steps.why_this_matters: Disk full, inode full, or OOM pressure are common Linux failure points and are more common than Apache rewrite problems.

steps.action: Check the main UI services. Confirm keycloak.service and ms-metro.service are running and ports 8443 and 8080 are open.
steps.decision_if_fail: Stop here and treat the missing service or listener as the current failure point.
evidence_to_collect: Disk, inode, memory, and OOM checks from df -h, df -ih, free -h, and journalctl -k.
Recent journalctl output for httpd.service, keycloak.service, and ms-metro.service.
steps.failure_point: keycloak.service, ms-metro.service, or their listener ports
steps.if_fail: Stop here and treat the missing service or listener as the current failure point.
steps.if_pass: If the services and listeners look healthy, go to the log checks.
steps.next_if_pass: If the services and listeners look healthy, go to the log checks.

recommended_commands.example_output:
● keycloak.service - The authentication and authorization server
   Loaded: loaded (/usr/lib/systemd/system/keycloak.service; enabled; vendor preset: disabled)
   Active: active (running) since Sat 2026-03-07 13:38:32 EST; 2 weeks 2 days ago
 Main PID: 3758 (algosec_keycloa)
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Unit is loaded and active (running).
recommended_commands.interpretation: If Keycloak is not running, the login path is failing before the UI can finish loading.
recommended_commands.label: Check keycloak.service status

recommended_commands.example_output:
● ms-metro.service - ms-metro Application Container
   Loaded: loaded (/etc/systemd/system/ms-metro.service; disabled; vendor preset: disabled)
   Active: active (running) since Sat 2026-03-07 13:39:32 EST; 2 weeks 2 days ago
 Main PID: 6012 (java)
recommended_commands.expected_signal: Unit is loaded and active (running).
recommended_commands.interpretation: If Metro is not running, the UI may open only partly or fail after the first page.
recommended_commands.label: Check ms-metro.service status

steps.why_this_matters: After Apache and host health, the next common failure is that a key Java service is down or not listening.

steps.action: Check the logs. Look for disk, memory, startup, or Java errors in httpd, keycloak, and ms-metro.
steps.decision_if_fail: Stop here and treat the service with the clear error as the current failure point.
evidence_to_collect: Disk, inode, memory, and OOM checks from df -h, df -ih, free -h, and journalctl -k.
Recent journalctl output for httpd.service, keycloak.service, and ms-metro.service.
steps.failure_point: Recent service errors in httpd, keycloak, or ms-metro logs
steps.if_fail: Stop here and treat the service with the clear error as the current failure point.
steps.if_pass: If the logs do not show the issue clearly, move to a narrower service or escalation workflow.
steps.next_if_pass: If the logs do not show the issue clearly, move to a narrower service or escalation workflow.

recommended_commands.expected_signal: No obvious disk, permission, startup, or crash errors appear in recent lines.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If recent httpd lines show errors, stay on Apache and follow that clue first.
recommended_commands.label: Review recent httpd logs

recommended_commands.expected_signal: No obvious startup, auth, memory, or crash errors appear in recent lines.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If recent Keycloak lines show errors, stay on Keycloak and follow that clue first.
recommended_commands.label: Review recent Keycloak logs

recommended_commands.expected_signal: No obvious Java heap, startup, disk, or application crash errors appear in recent lines.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If recent Metro lines show errors, stay on Metro and follow that clue first.
recommended_commands.label: Review recent Metro logs

steps.why_this_matters: Logs often show disk problems, heap problems, startup failures, and Java stack clues faster than config review.

decision_playbooks.symptom_focus: Use this when the customer UI is down and the engineer is checking the appliance from SSH.

decision_playbooks.decision_rule: If a step fails, stop the happy-path sequence, record that step as the current failure point, and collect the matching evidence before moving on.
decision_playbooks.label: FireFlow Backend

steps.action: Identify the failing FireFlow action and confirm that the symptom is tied to /FireFlow/api or /aff/api.
steps.decision_if_fail: If the symptom is not tied to FireFlow, switch to a different domain playbook instead of staying here.
evidence_to_collect: Result of the failing FireFlow action, including the exact endpoint or screen.
steps.if_fail: If the symptom is not tied to FireFlow, switch to a different domain playbook instead of staying here.
steps.if_pass: Continue to aff-boot and route validation.
steps.next_if_pass: Continue to aff-boot and route validation.

recommended_commands.expected_signal: The expected mapping token 1989 appears in /etc/httpd/conf.d/aff.conf.
recommended_commands.interpretation: Use the known proxy mapping to confirm the FireFlow path really terminates at the expected backend.
recommended_commands.label: Check config mapping

steps.action: Confirm aff-boot.service is active and that the failing FireFlow route still proxies to localhost:1989.
steps.decision_if_fail: Treat aff-boot.service or the 1989 route as the current failure point.
evidence_to_collect: Result of the failing FireFlow action, including the exact endpoint or screen.
Visible status for aff-boot.service and postgresql.service.
steps.if_fail: Treat aff-boot.service or the 1989 route as the current failure point.

recommended_commands.expected_signal: Unit is loaded and active (running).
recommended_commands.interpretation: If aff-boot is not active, stop here and treat the FireFlow service itself as the failure point.
recommended_commands.label: Check aff-boot.service status

recommended_commands.expected_signal: A listening socket is present for 1989.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If the FireFlow listener is missing, the backend route has nothing healthy to terminate to.
recommended_commands.label: Check FireFlow listener

recommended_commands.expected_signal: The expected mapping token 1989 appears in /etc/httpd/conf.d/aff.conf.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If the proxy config no longer points to 1989, the FireFlow request path may be broken even when the service is up.
recommended_commands.label: Check config mapping

steps.action: Confirm postgresql.service is active and local database access is healthy before assuming a FireFlow-only fault.
steps.decision_if_fail: Treat postgresql.service as the current failure point for this customer issue.
evidence_to_collect: Visible status for aff-boot.service and postgresql.service.
Any recent FireFlow-related lines from the customer-facing logs or service status output.
steps.if_fail: Treat postgresql.service as the current failure point for this customer issue.

recommended_commands.expected_signal: Unit is loaded and active (running).
recommended_commands.interpretation: If the database is not active, FireFlow symptoms are likely downstream of data availability.
recommended_commands.label: Check postgresql.service status

steps.action: Collect the latest FireFlow-related error lines or status output and decide whether the failure stays in FireFlow logic or must move deeper into dependencies.
steps.decision_if_fail: If you cannot collect the logs, escalate with the route, service, and database checks already captured.
evidence_to_collect: Visible status for aff-boot.service and postgresql.service.
Any recent FireFlow-related lines from the customer-facing logs or service status output.
steps.if_fail: If you cannot collect the logs, escalate with the route, service, and database checks already captured.

recommended_commands.expected_signal: Recent lines show healthy startup or a clear error signature to classify.
recommended_commands.interpretation: Look for permission errors, startup failures, or dependency errors that explain why FireFlow is unhealthy.
recommended_commands.label: Review aff-boot.service logs

decision_playbooks.symptom_focus: Use this when a FireFlow action fails or FireFlow returns an error.

decision_playbooks.decision_rule: If a step fails, stop the happy-path sequence, record that step as the current failure point, and collect the matching evidence before moving on.
decision_playbooks.label: Microservice Platform

steps.action: Identify the exact failing feature or ms-* path in the customer session so the diagnostic path stays anchored to one concrete product behavior.
steps.decision_if_fail: If the failing feature cannot be isolated, collect the customer symptom and stay at the domain level before picking a narrower path.
evidence_to_collect: Status of the routed path /ms-batch-application/local, /ms-batch-application and whether it still maps to 8159.
Status of the routed path /BusinessFlow/local, /BusinessFlow and whether it still maps to 8081.
steps.if_fail: If the failing feature cannot be isolated, collect the customer symptom and stay at the domain level before picking a narrower path.
steps.if_pass: Continue to wrapper and target-service validation.
steps.next_if_pass: Continue to wrapper and target-service validation.

steps.action: Confirm algosec-ms.service completed successfully and that the likely target service set is healthy: ms-batch-application.service, ms-bflow.service, ms-cloudlicensing.service.
steps.decision_if_fail: Treat algosec-ms.service or the first failing ms-* service as the current failure point.
evidence_to_collect: Status of the routed path /BusinessFlow/local, /BusinessFlow and whether it still maps to 8081.
Visible state of algosec-ms.service and ms-configuration.service.
steps.failure_point: algosec-ms.service or first failing ms-* service
steps.if_fail: Treat algosec-ms.service or the first failing ms-* service as the current failure point.

recommended_commands.expected_signal: Unit is loaded and active (running).
recommended_commands.interpretation: If this wrapper is unhealthy, start at the shared microservice platform before chasing one feature path.
recommended_commands.label: Check algosec-ms.service status

recommended_commands.expected_signal: Unit is loaded and active (running).
recommended_commands.interpretation: If the target ms-* service is unhealthy, treat that service as the current failure point.
recommended_commands.label: Check ms-batch-application.service status

steps.action: Confirm the customer-facing failing path still maps to the expected local target port for that microservice.
steps.decision_if_fail: Treat the routed path or local target port mapping as the current failure point.
evidence_to_collect: Status of the routed path /ms-batch-application/local, /ms-batch-application and whether it still maps to 8159.
Status of the routed path /BusinessFlow/local, /BusinessFlow and whether it still maps to 8081.
steps.failure_point: Routed path or local target port mapping
steps.if_fail: Treat the routed path or local target port mapping as the current failure point.

recommended_commands.expected_signal: A listening socket is present for 8159.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If the target port is missing, the feature path may be failing before the request reaches the service.
recommended_commands.label: Check microservice listener

recommended_commands.expected_signal: The expected mapping token 8159 appears in /etc/httpd/conf.d/algosec-ms.ms-batch-application.conf.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If the proxy mapping is wrong, the customer path may point to the wrong backend or no backend at all.
recommended_commands.label: Check config mapping

steps.action: Confirm ms-configuration.service is healthy before assuming the issue is isolated to the single feature path.
steps.decision_if_fail: Treat ms-configuration.service as the current failure point because other microservices depend on it.
evidence_to_collect: Visible state of algosec-ms.service and ms-configuration.service.
Any service status, console error, or log snippet for the failing ms-* feature path.
steps.if_fail: Treat ms-configuration.service as the current failure point because other microservices depend on it.
steps.if_pass: Continue to dependency and error review.
steps.next_if_pass: Continue to dependency and error review.

recommended_commands.expected_signal: Unit is loaded and active (running).
recommended_commands.interpretation: If configuration is unhealthy, many downstream ms-* services can fail together.
recommended_commands.label: Check ms-configuration.service status

steps.action: Collect the relevant ms-* service status or error snippet and decide whether the issue stays in the target service or moves to shared dependencies like httpd.service.
steps.decision_if_fail: If the error evidence cannot be collected, escalate with the routed-path and service-state checks already captured.
evidence_to_collect: Visible state of algosec-ms.service and ms-configuration.service.
Any service status, console error, or log snippet for the failing ms-* feature path.
steps.if_fail: If the error evidence cannot be collected, escalate with the routed-path and service-state checks already captured.
steps.if_pass: Inspect dependency services next: httpd.service, ms-configuration.service.
steps.next_if_pass: Inspect dependency services next: httpd.service, ms-configuration.service.

recommended_commands.expected_signal: Recent lines show healthy startup or a clear error signature to classify.
recommended_commands.interpretation: Use recent log lines to separate local service failure, permission issues, and deeper shared dependency problems.
recommended_commands.label: Review ms-batch-application.service logs

decision_playbooks.symptom_focus: Use this when one product feature fails after login and the UI itself is still up.

decision_playbooks.decision_rule: If a step fails, stop the happy-path sequence, record that step as the current failure point, and collect the matching evidence before moving on.
decision_playbooks.label: Messaging and Data

steps.action: Confirm the customer symptom is really a stuck job, stalled event, or data-backed action failure instead of a pure UI-path issue.
steps.decision_if_fail: If the symptom is UI-only, switch to UI and Proxy or Microservice Platform instead of staying here.
evidence_to_collect: Visible status of activemq.service and whether listener 61616 is present.
steps.if_fail: If the symptom is UI-only, switch to UI and Proxy or Microservice Platform instead of staying here.

steps.action: Confirm activemq.service is active and listener 61616 is present before assuming the issue is only downstream data state.
steps.decision_if_fail: Treat activemq.service or the 61616 listener as the current failure point.
evidence_to_collect: Visible status of activemq.service and whether listener 61616 is present.
Visible status of postgresql.service and any database connectivity error.
steps.if_fail: Treat activemq.service or the 61616 listener as the current failure point.

recommended_commands.expected_signal: Unit is loaded and active (running).
recommended_commands.interpretation: If the broker is not active, stop here and treat messaging infrastructure as the likely failure point.
recommended_commands.label: Check activemq.service status

recommended_commands.expected_signal: A listening socket is present for 61616.
recommended_commands.interpretation: If 61616 is missing, queued work may fail before any feature-specific processing begins.
recommended_commands.label: Check broker listener

steps.action: Confirm postgresql.service is active and that the customer environment shows no obvious database connectivity or state error.
steps.decision_if_fail: Treat postgresql.service as the current failure point for this issue.
evidence_to_collect: Visible status of postgresql.service and any database connectivity error.
Any stuck job, queue, or backlog indicator shown during the customer session.
steps.if_fail: Treat postgresql.service as the current failure point for this issue.
steps.if_pass: Continue to backlog or queue-state review.
steps.next_if_pass: Continue to backlog or queue-state review.

recommended_commands.expected_signal: Unit is loaded and active (running).
recommended_commands.interpretation: If the database is not active, treat data availability as the underlying failure point.
recommended_commands.label: Check postgresql.service status

steps.action: Review the visible queue, backlog, or stuck-job signal and decide whether the problem stays in broker/data infrastructure or should move back to a feature-specific domain.
steps.decision_if_fail: If queue or backlog evidence cannot be gathered, escalate with the broker and database checks already captured.
evidence_to_collect: Visible status of activemq.service and whether listener 61616 is present.
Visible status of postgresql.service and any database connectivity error.
Any stuck job, queue, or backlog indicator shown during the customer session.
steps.if_fail: If queue or backlog evidence cannot be gathered, escalate with the broker and database checks already captured.
steps.if_pass: If infrastructure checks passed, move to the feature-specific domain that owns the stalled job or action.
steps.next_if_pass: If infrastructure checks passed, move to the feature-specific domain that owns the stalled job or action.

recommended_commands.expected_signal: Recent lines show healthy startup or a clear error signature to classify.
recommended_commands.interpretation: Use broker log lines to decide whether the queue layer is blocked, degraded, or forwarding the issue downstream.
recommended_commands.label: Review activemq.service logs

recommended_commands.expected_signal: Recent lines show healthy startup or a clear error signature to classify.
recommended_commands.interpretation: Use database log lines to spot deeper availability or permission problems affecting queued work.
recommended_commands.label: Review postgresql.service logs

decision_playbooks.symptom_focus: Use this when jobs stop, queues back up, or data actions fail.

check_sequence: Confirm httpd.service is active and ports 80 and 443 are listening.
Check disk space, inode usage, and memory pressure before going deeper.
Confirm keycloak.service is active and listener 8443 is present.
Confirm ms-metro.service is active and listener 8080 is present.
Review recent httpd, keycloak, and ms-metro logs for startup, heap, disk, or permission errors.
evidence_to_collect_next: Output of systemctl status for httpd.service, keycloak.service, and ms-metro.service.
Listener output for ports 80, 443, 8443, and 8080 from ss -lntp.
Disk, inode, memory, and OOM checks from df -h, df -ih, free -h, and journalctl -k.
Recent journalctl output for httpd.service, keycloak.service, and ms-metro.service.
diagnostic_flows.label: Appliance UI is down
diagnostic_flows.symptom_focus: Use this when the customer UI is down and the engineer is checking the appliance from SSH.

check_sequence: Confirm aff-boot.service is active and /FireFlow/api or /aff/api still proxy to localhost:1989.
Confirm postgresql.service is active before treating FireFlow symptoms as application-only.
Confirm route or local target 1989 for aff-boot.service.
evidence_to_collect_next: Result of the failing FireFlow action, including the exact endpoint or screen.
Visible status for aff-boot.service and postgresql.service.
Any recent FireFlow-related lines from the customer-facing logs or service status output.
diagnostic_flows.label: FireFlow Backend
diagnostic_flows.symptom_focus: Use this when a FireFlow action fails or FireFlow returns an error.

check_sequence: Confirm algosec-ms.service completed successfully and the top ms-* services are active.
Compare the routed microservice paths first: ms-batch-application, ms-bflow, ms-cloudlicensing.
Check ms-configuration.service early because several other services depend on it.
Confirm route or local target 8159 for ms-batch-application.service.
Confirm route or local target 8081 for ms-bflow.service.
evidence_to_collect_next: Status of the routed path /ms-batch-application/local, /ms-batch-application and whether it still maps to 8159.
Status of the routed path /BusinessFlow/local, /BusinessFlow and whether it still maps to 8081.
Visible state of algosec-ms.service and ms-configuration.service.
Any service status, console error, or log snippet for the failing ms-* feature path.
diagnostic_flows.label: Microservice Platform
diagnostic_flows.next_dependency_focus: Inspect dependency services next: httpd.service, ms-configuration.service.
diagnostic_flows.symptom_focus: Use this when one product feature fails after login and the UI itself is still up.

check_sequence: Confirm activemq.service is active and listener 61616 is present.
Confirm postgresql.service is active and local database access is healthy.
evidence_to_collect_next: Visible status of activemq.service and whether listener 61616 is present.
Visible status of postgresql.service and any database connectivity error.
Any stuck job, queue, or backlog indicator shown during the customer session.
diagnostic_flows.label: Messaging and Data
diagnostic_flows.symptom_focus: Use this when jobs stop, queues back up, or data actions fail.

first_response_steps: Confirm the customer appliance hostname, OS family, and any visible build markers against the lab baseline.
Check whether the top AlgoSec services are active and enabled in the customer environment.
Compare Apache-routed endpoints and local listener ports before moving into deeper service-specific diagnosis.
Use the recorded config and log checkpoints for the top services before inferring broader platform issues.

support_focus: Start with AlgoSec product services and direct dependencies before generic Rocky platform services.
Use service state, local port bindings, and proxy-config mismatches before deeper inference when guiding a remote support session.

service_paths.label: aff-boot.service
service_paths.summary: route /FireFlow/api, /aff/api -> 1989; depends on postgresql.service; config checkpoints /etc/httpd/conf.d/aff.conf

service_paths.label: ms-batch-application.service
service_paths.summary: route /ms-batch-application/local, /ms-batch-application -> 8159; depends on httpd.service, ms-configuration.service; config checkpoints /etc/httpd/conf.d/algosec-ms.ms-batch-application.conf

service_paths.label: ms-bflow.service
service_paths.summary: route /BusinessFlow/local, /BusinessFlow -> 8081; depends on httpd.service; config checkpoints /etc/httpd/conf.d/algosec-ms.ms-bflow.conf, /etc/sysconfig/ms-bflow

service_paths.label: ms-cloudlicensing.service
service_paths.summary: route ms-cloudlicensing -> 8086; depends on httpd.service, ms-configuration.service; config checkpoints /etc/httpd/conf.d/algosec-ms.ms-cloudlicensing.conf

service_paths.label: ms-configuration.service
service_paths.summary: route ms-configuration -> 8185; depends on httpd.service; config checkpoints /etc/httpd/conf.d/algosec-ms.ms-configuration.conf

first_checks: Confirm httpd.service is active and ports 80 and 443 are listening.
Check disk space, inode usage, and memory pressure before going deeper.
Confirm keycloak.service is active and listener 8443 is present.
Confirm ms-metro.service is active and listener 8080 is present.
Review recent httpd, keycloak, and ms-metro logs for startup, heap, disk, or permission errors.
support_domains.label: Appliance UI is down

first_checks: Confirm aff-boot.service is active and /FireFlow/api or /aff/api still proxy to localhost:1989.
Confirm postgresql.service is active before treating FireFlow symptoms as application-only.
support_domains.label: FireFlow Backend
support_domains.summary: paths: aff-boot; services: aff-boot.service, algosec-dfs.service, postgresql.service

first_checks: Confirm algosec-ms.service completed successfully and the top ms-* services are active.
Compare the routed microservice paths first: ms-batch-application, ms-bflow, ms-cloudlicensing.
Check ms-configuration.service early because several other services depend on it.

support_domains.summary: paths: ms-batch-application, ms-bflow, ms-cloudlicensing, ms-configuration; services: algobot-microsoft-teams.service, algobot-slack.service, algobot-webex-teams.service, algosec-ms.service, elastic-ssh-tunnel.service, httpd.service

first_checks: Confirm activemq.service is active and listener 61616 is present.
Confirm postgresql.service is active and local database access is healthy.

symptom_lookup.first_action: Confirm httpd.service is active and ports 80 and 443 are listening.
Confirm httpd.service is active and ports 80 and 443 are listening.
Confirm aff-boot.service is active and /FireFlow/api or /aff/api still proxy to localhost:1989.
Confirm aff-boot.service is active and /FireFlow/api or /aff/api still proxy to localhost:1989.

unknowns: Only first-pass config and log checkpoints are collected in this slice.
Dependency mapping between AlgoSec services is not yet collected directly.
